ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Masala Vada; mapped to the closest parent class 'fritter (food product)' as a defensible approximate anchor. A more specific term for chana dal fritter does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Masala Vada. Mapped to legume-based deep-fried snack category as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Exact code for chana dal fritter not available in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Masala Vada or equivalent deep-fried lentil fritter as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Masala Vada consists of 2 pieces, weighing approximately 80–100g, commonly served as a tea-time snack with coconut chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutritional values for Masala Vada are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods', using chana dal (Bengal gram, split) as the primary base ingredient reference, adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption. Values for micronutrients are consistent with ICMR-NIN published data for chana dal-based preparations. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) for vegetable oil used in deep-frying.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Masala Vada as a composite dish. GI of 28 is estimated based on the GI of chana dal (Bengal gram, split, boiled) reported as approximately 8–28 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Deep-frying and the addition of spices and onion do not substantially alter the low GI of the chana dal base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (28 × 16.65) / 100 ≈ 4.7 for a 90g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg per 100g) and deep-fried preparation contributing to elevated caloric and fat load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Masala Vada to occasional consumption and restrict portion size to 1 piece, as the sodium content of 380 mg per 100g can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium limits of 2000 mg recommended by WHO.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~28) driven by chana dal base with high dietary fibre (7.5g per 100g) and plant-based protein (10.5g per 100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Masala Vada has a low GI of approximately 28 and a glycemic load of ~4.7 per 90g serving, making it a relatively blood-sugar-friendly snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions of 1–2 pieces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation results in elevated total fat (16g per 100g); however, cholesterol is negligible and dietary fibre is high (7.5g per 100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Masala Vada in moderation due to its deep-fried fat content; the high dietary fibre from chana dal may offer some cardiovascular benefit, but frequent consumption of deep-fried foods is not recommended by AHA guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying may produce pro-inflammatory compounds; lentils and fennel provide anti-inflammatory phytochemicals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Masala Vada occasionally, but should monitor personal tolerance to deep-fried pulses as the frying process can generate pro-inflammatory lipid oxidation products.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Masala Vada, also known as Paruppu Vadai or Masal Vadai, is a popular South Indian deep-fried snack made primarily from a coarsely ground paste of soaked chana dal (split Bengal gram / split chickpeas). The batter is seasoned with finely chopped onions, fresh curry leaves, green chillies, fennel seeds, ginger, and garlic, then shaped into flat discs and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden brown and crispy. The result is a fritter that is exceptionally crunchy on the outside and crumbly on the inside, with a savoury, spiced flavour profile. It is a staple street food and tea-time snack across Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Kerala, and is commonly served with coconut chutney or sambar. Nutritionally, Masala Vada provides approximately 260 kcal per 100g, with 10.5g protein, 16g fat, and 18.5g carbohydrates. Its most notable nutritional feature is its very high dietary fibre content (7.5g per 100g) from chana dal, which contributes to a very low glycemic index of approximately 28, making it one of the more blood-sugar-friendly deep-fried snacks. It is rich in folate (110 mcg), potassium (450 mg), magnesium (65 mg), and iron (2.8 mg). The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, it is suitable in moderation for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI, but sodium content (380 mg per 100g) warrants caution for those with hypertension. The deep-fried nature means it is calorie-dense and should be consumed in limited portions by those on weight-management or cardiovascular-risk-reduction diets.
common_user_queries: Is Masala Vada good for diabetics?
How many calories in Masala Vada?
What is the GI of Masala Vada?
What is the difference between Masala Vada and Medu Vada?

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN published data for chana dal-based preparations, which is an approved primary source for Indian foods. No exact entry for Masala Vada as a composite dish exists in ICMR-NIN; values are adapted from the chana dal base with adjustment for deep-frying oil absorption. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chana dal. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central for vegetable frying oil. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Masala Vada as a composite deep-fried preparation are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish as of 2024. Saturated fat (2.1g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for vegetable oil used in deep-frying at typical absorption rates. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: Masala Vada is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods, and vitamin D is naturally zero in this preparation as confirmed by ICMR-NIN data for legume-based dishes. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based vegan preparation. 3. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate based on chana dal (split, boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Masala Vada as a composite dish has been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. 4. Ontology codes are approximate; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry for Masala Vada exists as of 2024. 5. This is a South Indian dish, not a Singaporean dish; therefore Chinese, Malay, and full Singaporean multilingual requirements do not apply. Tamil and Hindi names are provided as the dish originates from South India and is widely consumed by Tamil and Hindi-speaking communities.
